source_label: BOLT-2: Peer Protocol for Channel Management (incl. splicing)
excerpt: The update_splice messages let the parties construct and sign a new funding transaction that spends the current funding output and creates a replacement output of a different size. The channel state continues across the splice: HTLCs in flight at the splice point are preserved, and routing through the channel does not need to pause beyond the time it takes to confirm the splicing transaction.
body:
Source excerpt:
The update_splice messages let the parties construct and sign a new funding transaction that spends the current funding output and creates a replacement output of a different size. The channel state continues across the splice: HTLCs in flight at the splice point are preserved, and routing through the channel does not need to pause beyond the time it takes to confirm the splicing transaction.

---

Cited by leaf "Splicing, fees, and operational realities":
A Lightning channel is a long-lived relationship, and the operational details — when to open, when to close, when to rebalance, what fees to charge — make the difference between a node that earns routing fees and a node whose capital sits idle. Channel *opening* is straightforward: a single on-chain transaction creates a 2-of-2 funding output. The capital is then committed for the lifetime of the channel; the only way to extract it is to close the channel back to the chain. Open-cost is the on-chain fee at the time of funding; this is the dominant economic constraint on channel sizing and is the reason small channels are economically marginal at any non-trivial fee rate [1].

*Splicing* (BOLT-2 `update_splice`, recently activated in production by several implementations) lets the parties add or remove capital from an existing channel via a single on-chain transaction without closing it. Before splicing, a node that wanted to add liquidity had to close the existing channel and open a new larger one — two on-chain transactions plus a window during which the channel was unroutable. After splicing, capital can be added in-place while the channel keeps forwarding payments. This is a substantial UX and capital-efficiency win, especially for LSP architectures that need to dynamically resize channels in response to user demand [2].

Channel *closing* has two flavours. *Cooperative close* — both parties agree on the final state and exchange a single mutually-signed transaction paying out the current balances. This is the cheapest and fastest path: one on-chain transaction, no timelocks, both outputs are spendable immediately. *Force close* — one party publishes the most recent commitment transaction unilaterally. The publishing party's main output is locked behind the `to_self_delay` (typically 144 to 1008 blocks) before it can be spent; the counterparty can spend immediately. Any HTLCs in flight at force-close time get cleaned up by HTLC-timeout or HTLC-success transactions, each subject to its own timelock. Force close is more expensive and slower than coop close, but it's the safety net that makes Lightning work when peers are unresponsive [3].

Forwarding fees are the income side. A routing node sets two values on each channel direction: a `base_fee_msat` (a flat sat-millisat charged per HTLC routed, regardless of size) and a `fee_rate_milli_msat` (a proportional fee in parts-per-million of the amount forwarded). The fees are asymmetric and per-direction — outbound fees on this channel apply only when this node is the *forwarding* hop, not when it is the source or destination of a payment. Tuning fees against expected demand and against the fees of nearby nodes is treated by the major routing operators as a continuous optimisation problem; tools like `charge-lnd` and Lightning Terminal automate part of it.

Channel *reliability* — keeping the peer online, monitoring HTLC stuck states, watching for counterparty force-close attempts, fee-bumping the on-chain anchor outputs when network fees spike — is operational work that small nodes typically delegate to LSP-provided turnkey runtimes (Voltage, Galoy Greenlight) or run on a dedicated home node like Umbrel or Start9. Routing-as-a-business at scale is a real engineering job; routing-as-a-hobby on a Raspberry Pi is a real possibility but with materially lower expected returns once on-chain fees are netted against forwarding income.

---

Cited by leaf "Payment channels and the funding transaction":
A Lightning payment channel is a 2-of-2 multisig output on the Bitcoin chain that two parties agree to treat as a private ledger between themselves. The channel is opened with a single on-chain *funding transaction* that locks bitcoin into the multisig; from that point onward the parties can update their balance allocation thousands of times by exchanging signed off-chain *commitment transactions*, none of which touch the Bitcoin chain unless the channel is closed [1].

Each commitment transaction is asymmetric. My version of the current state pays you immediately and pays me on a relative timelock (CSV); your version of the same state pays me immediately and pays you on a timelock. Whenever the parties agree on a new state, they exchange the per-commitment *revocation secret* for the previous state. If you ever broadcast an old commitment, I can use the revocation secret to sweep both your output and mine before your timelock expires — this is the *justice transaction* that keeps both parties honest without a third-party arbiter [2].

The happy path is a *cooperative close*: the parties agree on the final state and exchange a single mutually-signed transaction paying out the current balances. One on-chain transaction, no timelocks, both outputs spendable immediately. The unhappy path is a *force close*, used when one party is offline or uncooperative: the other broadcasts the most recent commitment unilaterally and waits out the `to_self_delay` window (typically 144 to 1008 blocks) before being able to spend its own output. HTLC outputs in flight at force-close time have their own cleanup paths via HTLC-timeout and HTLC-success transactions, each with its own timelock [3].

The funding transaction is what gives Lightning its security model. The worst case for any participant is that they end up settling on Bitcoin's chain with the most recent state they can prove. They never need to trust the counterparty with custody — only with availability long enough to exchange a final commitment, plus a mechanism (a watchtower, or simply being online) to punish breach attempts.

Channels can be unidirectional, bidirectional with explicit liquidity in both directions, or part of a routed payment graph where the channel's role is to forward payments for fees rather than to settle balance between the two endpoints. The economics of channel sizing — how much capital to commit, in which direction, and for how long — is the operational question that separates a routing node that earns fees from one whose capital sits idle.
